title: Chapter 34: First Contact
content: The Roosevelt Federal Building stood like a tombstone in the heart of the dead city.

Twelve stories of granite and glass, its lower floors barricaded with sandbags and overturned vehicles, its upper floors bristling with sentries and searchlights. The south entrance—the one the broadcast had specified—was a checkpoint of military precision: a chain-link gate, a scanning station, and two guards in riot gear whose body language said *we've seen things and we will end you if necessary*.

Luna and I approached from the south, hands visible, weapons holstered, moving slowly enough to communicate non-threat but quickly enough to avoid the zombies that prowled the streets around the building's perimeter.

"State your business," the taller guard called out, his rifle trained on my center mass. He was middle-aged, ex-military by the posture, with a face that had forgotten how to smile somewhere around Day Three.
"I heard your broadcast," I called back. "I'm a survivor from the north side. Crimson Ridge Complex, twelve blocks north. I'm here to talk."
"Talk about what?"
"About survival. About what's happening to the dead. About the systems on our wrists."
The guard's eyes flickered—just for a moment, a flash of recognition that confirmed everything I'd suspected. He knew about the system. He'd seen it before.
"Wait here," he said, and spoke into a radio clipped to his shoulder.

We waited. Five minutes. Ten. The zombies in the street kept their distance—something about the checkpoint's floodlights and constant noise repelled them, a lesson in applied terror that our group would do well to remember.
Then the gate opened, and a man stepped through.

He was tall—not as tall as Derek, but built with the same gym-sculpted confidence—though where Derek's physique had been a weapon for intimidation, this man's was armor for protection. He wore tactical pants, a black t-shirt, and a watch on his left wrist that glowed with the same soft blue-white light as mine.

His face was handsome in a weathered way—mid-thirties, strong jaw, grey eyes that assessed me with the cold efficiency of a man who'd made difficult decisions and slept fine afterward. His hair was cropped short, military-style, and his posture radiated the easy authority of someone who'd been in charge long before the apocalypse gave him an excuse.
"Alex Chen," he said, and it wasn't a question. "System host. Level 2—excuse me, Level 3 as of two days ago. Romance System variant. Impressive survival record."
My blood ran cold. "How do you know—"

"The System Network," he said, touching his watch. "All hosts are visible to each other once they're within detection range. I've known you were in this city since Day Twelve. I've been waiting for you to make contact."
The system had a *network*. Hosts could see each other. And this man had been tracking me for eighteen days without my knowledge.
"Who are you?" I asked.

"Colt Reeves. Former Army Rangers, current leader of the Roosevelt survivors, and host of the Command System." He held up his wrist, and the watch's display shifted to show a different interface—not the romance heart I was used to, but a战术 grid, all angles and data, that mapped the building behind him in real-time with red and green dots marking friendlies and hostiles.
"Command System," I repeated. "What does it reward?"

"Structure. Hierarchy. Efficient organization." His smile was polite and completely unreadable. "My system gives me tools to build a command structure—combat bonuses for my followers, resource allocation optimization, threat assessment algorithms. Where your system rewards emotional connection, mine rewards operational efficiency."
"And if those come into conflict?"

"They already have." He stepped closer, and I felt the watch on my wrist pulse—a warning, or maybe a greeting. "Your group operates on trust and love. Mine operates on command and control. Both work. Both have advantages. Both have... weaknesses."
"What do you want, Colt?"
The same question I'd been asked, and his answer was everything I'd feared and nothing I'd expected:
"I want to understand why we were chosen. I want to know who designed these systems and what they're for. And I want to know if your Romance System can do something my Command System can't."
"Which is?"

"Heal the dead." His eyes went to Luna, standing silent and watchful beside me. "Your system is reversing zombie degradation. Mine can only destroy them. You're doing something that shouldn't be possible, and the implications are bigger than either of us."

He was right, and he knew it, and the knowledge had been eating at him. I could see it in the tension around his eyes, the way his hand stayed near his watch, the faint tremor in his voice when he said *heal the dead*.
"Come inside," Colt said. "See what we've built. Then decide if there's room for cooperation between a heart and a command."
The Roosevelt's interior was a stark contrast to our titanium safe house.

Where we'd built a home—warm lighting, growing plants, shared meals—the Roosevelt had built a base. Barricaded hallways, armory stations on every floor, a mess hall with military precision. The survivors moved through the space with the disciplined efficiency of a unit that had been trained, not gathered.

I counted twenty-three people—far more than our eight—and they fell into clear categories: combat personnel, support staff, and leadership. It was a command structure, pure and simple, and it worked with the grinding reliability of a well-oiled machine.
"This is what the Command System builds," Colt said, watching my face as I took it in. "Efficiency. Survivability. But there's something it doesn't build."
"What?"

"Loyalty." He stopped walking and turned to face me. "My people follow me because the system rewards obedience. Your people follow you because they love you. When the system isn't looking, which kind of loyalty do you think holds?"

It was the most honest thing he'd said, and it told me more about Colt Reeves than any dossier could. He was a leader who'd built an efficient machine and discovered, too late, that machines don't love you back.
"Cooperation," I said. "Information sharing. Your military structure, our emotional bonds. Two approaches to the same problem."
"And the problem is?"

"The virus. The evolution. The systems. Whoever did this." I met his grey eyes with everything I had. "We're both trying to survive, Colt. We don't have to do it alone. And we don't have to do it the same way."
He considered this for a long moment, the command system's tactical readout glowing on his wrist while my romance system's heart pulsed on mine.
"I'll think about it," he said. "In the meantime, you're welcome to resupply here. Water, ammunition, non-perishables. Consider it a gesture of good faith."
"And in exchange?"

"Blood samples from your recovering zombie. Dr. Kim's research. Everything you know about the Romance System's food rewards and their effect on viral degradation." His expression hardened. "I have a doctor too—Dr. Harmon. He's been studying the virus since Day One, and he's hit a wall. Your zombie's antibodies might be the key he needs."
Luna's blood. Rose's research. The core of our group's most valuable asset, offered in trade.
"I can't make that decision alone," I said. "The data belongs to Rose—and Luna's blood is hers to give or refuse."
"Of course." Colt nodded, as if he'd expected nothing less. "Bring your people. Let them see what we've built. Let them choose."

It was a fair offer. More than fair—it was generous, by apocalypse standards. But somewhere in the back of my mind, a voice that sounded like Mia's was whispering: *This is too easy. He's too reasonable. There's a hook.*
I'd find the hook later. For now, I had a partnership to consider, a group to consult, and a system that was quietly vibrating with a notification I hadn't read yet.

**QUEST UPDATE: THE OTHER HOST**
**STATUS: Contact Established ✓**
**HOST: Colt Reeves — Command System**
**AFFECTION: 0% (Not a romance target — same-gender hosts are not romantically compatible with your system)**
**SYSTEM INTELLIGENCE: Command System rewards hierarchy, obedience, and tactical efficiency. Host is competent but emotionally isolated. Alliance potential: MODERATE. Trust level: LOW.**
Not a romance target. The system had confirmed what I'd suspected—Colt wasn't a conquest, he was a complication. A powerful, necessary, dangerous complication.

We left the Roosevelt as the sun set, carrying supplies and uncertainty in equal measure. Luna was quiet on the return journey—quieter than usual—and when I asked her what was wrong, she said something that stayed with me for the rest of the trip:
"His eyes looked like mine. Before you fed me. Hungry and empty."
She was right. Colt Reeves was a system host with every advantage—power, resources, followers—and none of the things that made those advantages mean something.
He was efficient. He was organized. He was alone.
Just like I'd been, before a zombie girl taught me the word *stay*.
